title: Data Architecture
description: Splitting data and styling between JSON and TSX for cleaner, more maintainable applications.
keyPoints: Hardcoding data inside components becomes painful as projects grow — separation is the answer
JSON files store the WHAT (content, questions, topics) while TSX files handle the HOW (layout, styling, interactions)
The backend is the home for your data — the frontend asks for it through an API
TypeScript interfaces act as a contract between your JSON data and your frontend code
Use local JSON for small, static data and a backend API for large, dynamic, or shared data

blocks.body: This page explains one of the most important patterns in modern web development: keeping your data (what you show) separate from your display code (how you show it). If you have ever struggled to update content buried deep inside a component, this pattern will change your life.

blocks.title: 1. The Problem: Mixed Data and Code

blocks.body: When you first start building a React app, it feels natural to put everything in one place. Your component has the layout, the styling, and the data all mashed together. It works — until it does not.

blocks.heading: A "bad" example — data hardcoded into the component

blocks.code:
function QuizPage() { return (

Ham Radio Quiz

{/* Question 1 is hardcoded right here */}

Question 1

What does the acronym "RF" stand for?

  • Radio Frequency
  • Resonant Filter
  • Rapid Feedback
  • Register Function

Correct answer: Radio Frequency

{/* Question 2 is also hardcoded */}

Question 2

What is the purpose of an antenna?

  • To convert electrical signals to radio waves
  • To amplify sound
  • To store energy
  • To measure voltage

Correct answer: To convert electrical signals to radio waves

{/* Imagine 400 more questions here... */}
); }

cards.title: Maintenance nightmare
cards.body: Imagine you have 400 quiz questions. To fix a typo in question 347, you have to scroll through hundreds of lines of JSX.

cards.title: No reuse
cards.body: Want to show the same questions on a mobile app? You would have to copy-paste the entire component. Change one question, and now you need to update it in two places.

cards.title: Messy version control
cards.body: When a content editor changes a question and a developer changes the styling in the same file, you get merge conflicts even though the changes are unrelated.

cards.title: Hard to test
cards.body: You cannot test the data separately from the display. If a test fails, is it the data or the component logic?

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: Hardcoding data inside your components is fine for a quick prototype but becomes painful the moment your project grows. The answer is separation — keep data in one place and display logic in another.

blocks.title: 2. The Solution: JSON for Data, TSX for Display

blocks.body: The core idea is simple: JSON files store the WHAT (content, questions, topics, navigation items) and TSX files handle the HOW (layout, styling, interactions, animations).

Think of a restaurant. The menu (JSON) lists every dish, its price, and its ingredients. The decor (TSX) decides whether the menu is printed on paper, displayed on a chalkboard, or shown on a tablet. You can change the menu without repainting the walls, and you can redecorate without touching the food.

rows: Content is added, corrected, or removed

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: Separating data (JSON) from display (TSX) makes your project easier to maintain, easier to collaborate on, and easier to scale. Each file has one job.

In the hamradiosite pattern, JSON data files live in the backend, typically inside backend/data/ or backend/seeds/. The frontend never stores its own copy of the data — instead, it fetches what it needs through the API.

blocks.code:
my-project/
├── backend/
│   ├── data/                   # <-- JSON data lives here
│   │   ├── topics.json
│   │   ├── questions.json
│   │   └── navigation.json
│   ├── seeds/                  # <-- Or here for database seeding
│   │   └── seed-questions.json
│   ├── routes/
│   │   └── questions.ts        # API endpoint serves the JSON
│   └── server.ts
│
└── frontend/
    ├── src/
    │   ├── api/
    │   │   └── client.ts       # Fetches data from the backend
    │   ├── components/
    │   │   └── QuestionCard.tsx # Displays the data
    │   └── types/
    │       └── question.ts     # TypeScript interface for the data
    └── package.json

blocks.heading: Why keep JSON in the backend?

cards.title: Single source of truth
cards.body: There is exactly one place where questions live. No risk of the frontend and backend having different versions.

cards.title: Security
cards.body: Correct answers stay on the server. The frontend only receives what it needs for each request.

cards.title: Multiple consumers
cards.body: A React web app, a React Native mobile app, and an admin dashboard can all fetch from the same API.

cards.title: Easy updates
cards.body: A content editor can update a JSON file in the backend repo without ever opening the frontend code.

blocks.body: Common beginner mistake: Copying JSON files into the frontend's public/ folder. This works for prototypes, but in production your data should live behind an API so you can control access, filter results, and paginate large datasets.

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: The backend is the home for your data. The frontend asks for data when it needs it, displays it, and never tries to be the permanent storage.
Why go through the trouble of splitting things up? Because the benefits compound as your project grows.

cards.title: Non-developers can update content
cards.body: A subject-matter expert can open questions.json, fix a typo, or add a new question without knowing any React. JSON is just text with a predictable format.

cards.title: Multiple frontends share the same data
cards.body: Your website, your mobile app, and your admin panel all call the same API. Update the data once and every client sees the change.

cards.title: Easier testing
cards.body: You can validate JSON data with a simple schema check (are all required fields present?). You can test components by passing in mock data. Each side is tested independently.

cards.title: Data is portable
cards.body: JSON is a universal format. You can import it into a database, export it to a spreadsheet, or feed it to another system. Your data is never trapped inside a React component.

cards.title: Cleaner version control
cards.body: When data changes and code changes live in separate files, Git diffs are cleaner. A content update only touches JSON files. A design update only touches TSX files. No merge conflicts between unrelated changes.

cards.title: Performance optimization
cards.body: With data in the backend, you can add pagination, caching, and search without changing the frontend. Serve 10 questions at a time instead of loading all 400.

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: Separation of data and display is not just a nice pattern — it is a force multiplier. It makes your project easier to maintain, share, test, and grow.
One giant JSON file with everything in it becomes just as hard to maintain as a giant component. The solution is to split your data across multiple files and link them with IDs and slugs.

blocks.heading: questions.json — links back to topic slugs
How the files reference each other

blocks.code:
navigation.json                topics.json               questions.json
  ┌──────────────┐         ┌──────────────────┐       ┌──────────────────┐
  │ path:        │         │ slug: "basics"   │       │ id: "q1"         │
  │ "/topics/    │────────>│                  │       │ topicSlug:       │
  │   basics"    │         │ questionIds:     │──────>│   "basics"       │
  └──────────────┘         │   ["q1","q2"...] │       │                  │
                           └──────────────────┘       └──────────────────┘
                                    ^                          │
                                    │      topicSlug           │
                                    └──────────────────────────┘

  Navigation tells you WHERE to go.
  Topics tell you WHAT is covered and WHICH questions belong.
  Questions tell you the ACTUAL content and link back to their topic.

blocks.body: Tip: Use slugs (like "basics") for human-readable references and IDs (like "q1") for machine references. Slugs work well in URLs. IDs work well in code.

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: Keep JSON file sizes manageable by splitting data into logical files. Use IDs and slugs so files can reference each other without duplicating content.

JSON is flexible — a little too flexible. Nothing stops you from accidentally writing "corectAnswer" instead of "correctAnswer". TypeScript interfaces act as a contract: they define exactly what shape your data should have, and your editor will warn you if something does not match.

blocks.heading: Define interfaces that match your JSON

cards.title: Autocomplete
cards.body: Your editor suggests field names as you type. No more guessing whether it is "answer" or "correctAnswer".

cards.title: Catch mistakes early
cards.body: Typos, missing fields, and wrong types are flagged before you even run the code.

cards.title: Living documentation
cards.body: New team members can look at the interface file to understand the data structure instantly.

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: Always create TypeScript interfaces that mirror your JSON data. They give you autocomplete, catch typos, and serve as documentation for your data shapes.
Now that the data lives in the backend and you have TypeScript interfaces, you need a way to fetch that data in the frontend. You can use the built-in fetch function or a library like axios.

blocks.heading: A simple API client with axios
Using it in a component with loading states

blocks.body: Three states to always handle: (1) Loading — show a spinner. (2) Error — show a helpful message. (3) Empty — show a "nothing here" message. Never leave the user staring at a blank screen.

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: Create a centralized API client with interceptors for auth and error handling. Always handle loading, error, and empty states in your components.
Let us trace a quiz question from its origin in a JSON file all the way to appearing on the user's screen. This is the full data flow in action.

blocks.title: JSON question in the backend
blocks.body: The data starts as a JSON object in a file on the server.

blocks.title: API endpoint serves the data
blocks.body: An Express route reads the JSON and sends it to any client that asks.

blocks.title: Frontend fetches via API client
blocks.body: The frontend uses axios to request questions from the API.

blocks.title: Redux slice stores the state
blocks.body: The fetched data is stored in a Redux slice for the rest of the app to use.

blocks.title: QuestionCard component displays it
blocks.body: A presentational component receives a question object and renders it.

blocks.title: The user sees the question
blocks.body: The page component wires everything together — dispatching the fetch, reading from Redux, and rendering the QuestionCard.

blocks.heading: The complete flow at a glance

blocks.code:
questions.json ──> API endpoint ──> API client ──> Redux store ──> Component ──> Screen
   (backend)       (Express)      (axios)      (quizSlice)  (QuestionCard)  (user)

   THE DATA       SERVES IT     FETCHES IT    STORES IT    RENDERS IT     SEES IT

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: Data flows in one direction: from the JSON source, through the API, into the frontend state, and finally onto the screen. Each layer has a single responsibility, making the system easy to understand, debug, and maintain.

blocks.title: 9. When to Use Local JSON vs API

blocks.body: Not everything needs to go through an API. Some data is so small and static that keeping it in a local JSON file inside the frontend is perfectly fine. Here is how to decide.

rows: Changes frequently or by multiple people
Multiple apps need the same data

blocks.body: Rule of thumb: If you would be comfortable pasting the data directly into your component and it would not make the file unreasonably long, local JSON is fine. If the data feels like it belongs in a database, use an API.

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.body: Use local JSON for small, static, frontend-only data like navigation and glossary terms. Use a backend API for large, dynamic, or shared data like quiz questions and user content. When in doubt, start local and move to an API when you outgrow it.

blocks.heading: Summary
blocks.body: JSON holds the data. TSX handles the display. The backend serves the data through an API. TypeScript interfaces keep everything in sync. This separation makes your projects easier to maintain, test, collaborate on, and scale. Start small with local JSON files and graduate to a full API as your project grows.
